title: Treen care and restoration
subtitle: Cleaning, re-oiling, and repairing small carved wooden objects
excerpt: A guide to maintaining and restoring treen: the small carved and turned wooden domestic objects that include spoons, bowls, ladles, and boxes. Covers cleaning, re-oiling schedules, repairing surface cracks, and stripping and re-finishing an old piece.
sourceNotes: Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the maintenance of domestic wooden utensils. Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on treen and wooden domestic objects.

glossaryTerms.definition: Small domestic objects made from wood by carving or turning: spoons, bowls, ladles, butter moulds, and similar household items.
Air-dried wood at stable moisture content. Treen care aims to keep the wood at a stable moisture level rather than allowing it to cycle between wet and dry.

content.text: Treen is the term for small wooden domestic objects: spoons, bowls, ladles, platters, and similar items made by carving or turning.

lasts for generations with straightforward care. Most problems with old wooden objects come from neglect (the wood dries out, checks, and splits) or from the wrong kind of cleaning (repeated wetting in hot water, which raises and breaks down the surface fibres).

For food-contact treen (spoons, bowls, boards), wash by hand in cool or warm water with minimal soap, dry immediately with a cloth, and stand the piece on its edge or hang it to complete drying. Do not leave wooden objects sitting in water. Do not put them in the dishwasher; the heat and prolonged wetting opens the grain, raises the surface, and eventually splits the piece.

Wooden utensils need re-oiling when the surface looks dry, pale, or dull rather than warm and slightly lustrous. For daily-use pieces, re-oil two to four times per year. Apply a thin coat of raw linseed oil, walnut oil, or board butter with a cloth, allow it to soak in overnight, then wipe off any excess. A piece that absorbs oil instantly and still looks dry needs a second coat after twenty-four hours.

Hairline checks at the rim of a bowl or along the handle of a spoon that do not go through the wall are normal. They stop once the wood reaches its equilibrium moisture content. Do not fill them with glue; the movement that opened the crack will reopen a glued crack. Oil the piece well; the oil reduces the rate of moisture cycling that causes the crack to flex.

A surface that feels rough after washing has had its grain raised by the water. Sand lightly with 400-grit paper along the grain when the piece is fully dry. Wipe off the dust and re-oil. After two or three cycles of wetting, drying, sanding, and oiling, the surface stabilises and no longer raises after washing.
Stripping and re-finishing an old piece
Old treen with a failed varnish or cracked paint finish can be stripped back and re-oiled. Remove the old finish by sanding, working from 120 grit to 240 grit to 400 grit. Old

wood sands easily once the surface finish is broken through. Wipe off all sanding dust and apply a first coat of raw linseed oil immediately. The bare wood will absorb the oil quickly; repeat until the surface is no longer thirsty.

A crack that runs through the full wall of a bowl or through the neck of a spoon is structural and cannot be safely glued for food use; the stress that opened the crack will reopen it. Set these pieces aside for decorative use or compost. Deep mould staining that has penetrated below the surface cannot be sanded out without removing too much material. A surface that has been deeply scored by a knife on a cutting board can be sanded back if the depth of the marks is within the wall thickness; otherwise the piece is past restoration.
